summary: Gruber responds to Matt Birchler's argument that people mistake familiarity for intuitiveness when comparing Macs and iPads. He clarifies that his criticism of iPadOS is not about intuitiveness at all — he actually agrees the iPad's touch-based direct manipulation is inherently more intuitive than the Mac's mouse-driven interface. His real complaints center on iPadOS's lack of consistency, coherence, and discoverability, particularly in multitasking gestures that require unnecessary precision. He argues that iPadOS squanders its natural advantage by hiding functionality behind inscrutable gestures rather than exposing it through tappable visual elements. The piece culminates in a call for iPadOS to embrace visible affordances rather than gesture-heavy hidden interfaces.
key_points: Gruber's iPad criticism is about consistency, coherence, and discoverability — not intuitiveness, which the iPad actually wins on by nature of direct touch manipulation
iPadOS multitasking requires more finicky dexterity than Mac multitasking, which is the opposite of what should be true given the iPad's conceptual foundation
The Mac's complexity is almost entirely discoverable visually through menus and clickable elements, while iPadOS hides advanced features behind non-obvious gestures
Most users would be better served by iOS than macOS, but iPadOS file management is somehow worse than the Mac's already-overwhelming approach
Full-screen mode feels bolted-on for the Mac but is the iPad's true nature — another missed opportunity for iPadOS to excel
money_quotes: The problems with the iPad are about consistency, coherence, and discoverability.
That's what makes the state of iPadOS so crushingly disappointing — it has an inherent leg up on MacOS on intuitiveness by nature of its conceptual foundation.
iPadOS should be less finicky than MacOS, but all of the multitasking features are the other way around.
The Mac interface for managing files is too overwhelming for typical users to understand, but somehow iPadOS offers something worse.
Far more of iPadOS should be exposed by visual buttons and on-screen elements that you can look at and simply tap or drag with a single finger.
I strongly believe most people would be better off with iOS as their main computer than MacOS — including most Mac users today.
Direct manipulation with touch vs. indirect manipulation via mouse pointer is clearly far more intuitive and natural.
key_insight: The iPad's problem isn't that it lacks intuitiveness — it's that it squanders its inherent intuitiveness advantage by hiding features behind inconsistent, undiscoverable gestures instead of exposing them through visible affordances.
